main_paper.raw: {"methods":["LLM-mediated interventions for MARL systems","Natural Language (NL) Controller using LLMs to simulate human-like interventions","Rule-Based (RB) Controller for interventions","Real-time, adaptive interventions during training"],"problems":["Efficient learning and desirable behaviour in complex multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) systems","Reward misspecification and reward hacking in MARL","Adapting to changing environments or goals in MARL","Enabling intuitive, non-expert user steering of MARL systems via natural language","Improving coordination and decision-making in dynamic, high-stakes environments"],"datasets":["Aerial Wildfire Suppression (AWS) environment from the HIVEX suite"],"metrics":["Training efficiency (speed to expert-level performance)","Agent performance (task success, coordination)"],"results":[{"metric":"Training efficiency (time to expert-level performance)","value":"Interventions, especially early in training, accelerate learning compared to baseline"},{"metric":"Agent performance","value":"Both NL and RB controllers outperform baseline (no intervention); NL controller has stronger impact than RB controller"}],"novelty_claims":["First demonstration of LLM-mediated natural language interventions to guide multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) systems in complex environments","Introduction of a system allowing both rule-based and natural language-based real-time interventions for MARL","Adaptive, dynamic guidance that goes beyond static curriculum-based methods, enabling real-time user steering of MARL systems","Application and validation of these methods in the challenging AWS environment, simulating real-world wildfire management"]}

methods: LLM-mediated interventions for MARL systems
Natural Language (NL) Controller using LLMs to simulate human-like interventions
Rule-Based (RB) Controller for interventions
Real-time, adaptive interventions during training
problems: Efficient learning and desirable behaviour in complex multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) systems
Reward misspecification and reward hacking in MARL
Adapting to changing environments or goals in MARL
Enabling intuitive, non-expert user steering of MARL systems via natural language
Improving coordination and decision-making in dynamic, high-stakes environments
datasets: Aerial Wildfire Suppression (AWS) environment from the HIVEX suite

results.value: Interventions, especially early in training, accelerate learning compared to baseline
Both NL and RB controllers outperform baseline (no intervention); NL controller has stronger impact than RB controller

novelty_claims: First demonstration of LLM-mediated natural language interventions to guide multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) systems in complex environments
Introduction of a system allowing both rule-based and natural language-based real-time interventions for MARL
Adaptive, dynamic guidance that goes beyond static curriculum-based methods, enabling real-time user steering of MARL systems
Application and validation of these methods in the challenging AWS environment, simulating real-world wildfire management

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":[],"problems":["How to extend LLM-based Reinforcement Learning (RL) to Multi-Agent Systems (MAS), considering coordination and communication between agents.","How to leverage human knowledge and natural language in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) for improved communication and cooperation.","How to design language-conditioned MARL frameworks that enable agents to communicate and coordinate using linguistic cues."],"datasets":[],"metrics":[],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["This paper is among the first to provide a systematic overview of the LLM-based Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) problem.","The paper focuses specifically on cooperative tasks in MAS using LLMs, which has not been the emphasis of previous surveys.","The paper discusses potential future research directions for LLM-based MARL, a topic not previously dedicated in the literature."]}

problems: How to extend LLM-based Reinforcement Learning (RL) to Multi-Agent Systems (MAS), considering coordination and communication between agents.
How to leverage human knowledge and natural language in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) for improved communication and cooperation.
How to design language-conditioned MARL frameworks that enable agents to communicate and coordinate using linguistic cues.
novelty_claims: This paper is among the first to provide a systematic overview of the LLM-based Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) problem.
The paper focuses specifically on cooperative tasks in MAS using LLMs, which has not been the emphasis of previous surveys.
The paper discusses potential future research directions for LLM-based MARL, a topic not previously dedicated in the literature.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Multi-Agent Transformer (MAT): an encoder-decoder architecture that casts cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) as a sequence modeling problem using the multi-agent advantage decomposition theorem and sequential update scheme; trained online in an on-policy fashion"],"problems":["How to abstract multi-agent decision making into a sequence modeling (SM) problem to leverage the power of modern sequence models","Improving cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) by addressing the complexity of agent interactions and joint policy optimization","Overcoming limitations of prior MARL methods that fail to guarantee monotonic joint performance improvement and require handcrafted objectives"],"datasets":["StarCraftII","Multi-Agent MuJoCo","Dexterous Hands Manipulation","Google Research Football"],"metrics":["Performance (e.g., win rate, return, or task-specific score)","Data efficiency","Few-shot generalization ability"],"results":[{"metric":"Performance vs. MAPPO, HAPPO, QMIX, UPDeT","value":"MAT achieves superior performance on all benchmarks"},{"metric":"Data efficiency","value":"MAT demonstrates higher data efficiency compared to strong baselines"},{"metric":"Few-shot generalization","value":"MAT is an excellent few-shot learner on unseen tasks, regardless of agent number"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a novel architecture (MAT) that formulates cooperative MARL as a sequence modeling problem, enabling the use of powerful sequence models for MARL","Introduces an encoder-decoder architecture leveraging the multi-agent advantage decomposition theorem for efficient and monotonic joint policy optimization in MARL","MAT is trained online (on-policy) rather than relying on offline pre-collected data, unlike prior sequence model-based RL methods such as Decision Transformer","Demonstrates state-of-the-art performance and strong generalization (few-shot learning) on standard multi-agent benchmarks"]}

methods: Multi-Agent Transformer (MAT): an encoder-decoder architecture that casts cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) as a sequence modeling problem using the multi-agent advantage decomposition theorem and sequential update scheme; trained online in an on-policy fashion
problems: How to abstract multi-agent decision making into a sequence modeling (SM) problem to leverage the power of modern sequence models
Improving cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) by addressing the complexity of agent interactions and joint policy optimization
Overcoming limitations of prior MARL methods that fail to guarantee monotonic joint performance improvement and require handcrafted objectives
metrics: Performance (e.g., win rate, return, or task-specific score)

results.metric: Performance vs. MAPPO, HAPPO, QMIX, UPDeT
results.value: MAT achieves superior performance on all benchmarks
MAT demonstrates higher data efficiency compared to strong baselines
MAT is an excellent few-shot learner on unseen tasks, regardless of agent number

novelty_claims: Proposes a novel architecture (MAT) that formulates cooperative MARL as a sequence modeling problem, enabling the use of powerful sequence models for MARL
Introduces an encoder-decoder architecture leveraging the multi-agent advantage decomposition theorem for efficient and monotonic joint policy optimization in MARL
MAT is trained online (on-policy) rather than relying on offline pre-collected data, unlike prior sequence model-based RL methods such as Decision Transformer
Demonstrates state-of-the-art performance and strong generalization (few-shot learning) on standard multi-agent benchmarks

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["SMACv2 benchmark with procedurally generated scenarios","Extended Partial Observability (EPO) challenge in SMACv2","Ablation studies on observation features","Evaluation of state-of-the-art MARL algorithms on SMACv2"],"problems":["SMAC benchmark lacks sufficient stochasticity and partial observability, making it too easy for current algorithms","Open-loop policies can achieve high win rates in SMAC, indicating insufficient challenge for closed-loop policy learning","Need for a benchmark that requires generalisation to unseen scenarios and meaningful decentralised coordination"],"datasets":["SMACv2 (procedurally generated StarCraft II scenarios)","Original SMAC benchmark (for comparison)"],"metrics":["Win rate","Regression error to joint Q-function"],"results":[{"metric":"Regression error to joint Q-function (open-loop policy on SMAC)","value":"<10% error in all but two scenarios using only timestep information"},{"metric":"Win rate (open-loop policy on SMAC)","value":"Non-trivial win rates on many SMAC scenarios"},{"metric":"Win rate (state-of-the-art algorithms on SMACv2)","value":"Struggle with many scenarios; significant drop compared to SMAC"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of SMACv2, a new benchmark for cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning with procedurally generated scenarios to increase stochasticity and require generalisation","Introduction of the Extended Partial Observability (EPO) challenge to ensure meaningful partial observability and decentralised coordination","Demonstration that open-loop policies perform well on SMAC, revealing fundamental limitations of the original benchmark","SMACv2 requires closed-loop policies and presents new challenges not present in SMAC, as evidenced by the poor performance of state-of-the-art algorithms"]}

methods: SMACv2 benchmark with procedurally generated scenarios
Extended Partial Observability (EPO) challenge in SMACv2
Ablation studies on observation features
Evaluation of state-of-the-art MARL algorithms on SMACv2
problems: SMAC benchmark lacks sufficient stochasticity and partial observability, making it too easy for current algorithms
Open-loop policies can achieve high win rates in SMAC, indicating insufficient challenge for closed-loop policy learning
Need for a benchmark that requires generalisation to unseen scenarios and meaningful decentralised coordination

results.metric: Regression error to joint Q-function (open-loop policy on SMAC)
results.value: <10% error in all but two scenarios using only timestep information
Non-trivial win rates on many SMAC scenarios
Struggle with many scenarios; significant drop compared to SMAC

novelty_claims: Introduction of SMACv2, a new benchmark for cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning with procedurally generated scenarios to increase stochasticity and require generalisation
Introduction of the Extended Partial Observability (EPO) challenge to ensure meaningful partial observability and decentralised coordination
Demonstration that open-loop policies perform well on SMAC, revealing fundamental limitations of the original benchmark
SMACv2 requires closed-loop policies and presents new challenges not present in SMAC, as evidenced by the poor performance of state-of-the-art algorithms

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Using large language models (LLMs) as proxy reward functions for RL via natural language prompts (few-shot or zero-shot)","General RL training framework leveraging LLM-based proxy rewards, agnostic to RL algorithm","Prompting LLMs with user-specified objectives to generate reward signals for RL agents"],"problems":["Difficulty of reward design in RL to capture human preferences","High cost and impracticality of collecting large amounts of labeled data for reward learning","Lack of intuitive interfaces for users to specify desired agent behaviors","Generalization of reward functions to new user objectives"],"datasets":["Ultimatum Game","Matrix Games","DealOrNoDeal negotiation task"],"metrics":["Alignment with user objectives (measured by human ratings)","Objective alignment accuracy (quantitative comparison to baseline)","Statistical significance (p-value)"],"results":[{"metric":"Objective alignment accuracy improvement over baseline","value":"35% average improvement"},{"metric":"Human user alignment rating (pilot study, n=10)","value":"Significantly higher for LLM-trained agent, p < 0.001"}],"novelty_claims":["First to introduce the use of LLMs as proxy reward functions for RL via natural language prompts","Propose a general RL training framework that is agnostic to the RL algorithm and leverages LLM-based proxy rewards","Demonstrate that LLMs can train RL agents to be more accurately aligned with user objectives than supervised reward learning, using only a few examples or descriptions","Provide empirical analysis of data efficiency and prompt sensitivity in LLM-based reward design"]}

results.metric: Human user alignment rating (pilot study, n=10)
results.value: Significantly higher for LLM-trained agent, p < 0.001

novelty_claims: First to introduce the use of LLMs as proxy reward functions for RL via natural language prompts

Provide empirical analysis of data efficiency and prompt sensitivity in LLM-based reward design

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["QMIX (Monotonic Value Function Factorisation)","Mixing network with monotonicity constraint using non-negative weights","Hypernetworks to condition mixing network weights on global state","Centralised training with decentralised execution paradigm"],"problems":["Learning decentralised policies for cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning under partial observability and communication constraints","Extracting decentralised policies from centralised value functions","Scalability of joint action-value function learning with many agents","Lack of challenging standard benchmarks for centralised training with decentralised execution in multi-agent RL"],"datasets":["StarCraft Multi-Agent Challenge (SMAC)"],"metrics":["Win rate (percentage of games won)","Sample efficiency","Learning speed"],"results":[{"metric":"Win rate (SMAC scenarios)","value":"QMIX significantly outperforms IQL, VDN, and COMA, especially on harder tasks and with heterogeneous agents"},{"metric":"Learning speed","value":"QMIX learns faster than IQL, VDN, and COMA on SMAC tasks"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes QMIX, a novel value-based method for deep multi-agent RL that factorises the joint action-value function as a monotonic combination of per-agent value functions, enabling decentralised policies to be extracted from centralised training.","Introduces a mixing network with non-negative weights to enforce monotonicity between joint and per-agent value functions, guaranteeing consistency between centralised and decentralised policies.","Uses hypernetworks to condition the mixing network on global state information available only during training, allowing richer representations than previous methods like VDN.","Introduces the StarCraft Multi-Agent Challenge (SMAC) as a new, challenging benchmark for deep multi-agent RL with decentralised execution.","Open-sources PyMARL, a modular and extensible framework for deep multi-agent RL research."]}

methods: QMIX (Monotonic Value Function Factorisation)
Mixing network with monotonicity constraint using non-negative weights
Hypernetworks to condition mixing network weights on global state
Centralised training with decentralised execution paradigm
problems: Learning decentralised policies for cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning under partial observability and communication constraints
Extracting decentralised policies from centralised value functions
Scalability of joint action-value function learning with many agents
Lack of challenging standard benchmarks for centralised training with decentralised execution in multi-agent RL

results.value: QMIX significantly outperforms IQL, VDN, and COMA, especially on harder tasks and with heterogeneous agents
QMIX learns faster than IQL, VDN, and COMA on SMAC tasks

novelty_claims: Proposes QMIX, a novel value-based method for deep multi-agent RL that factorises the joint action-value function as a monotonic combination of per-agent value functions, enabling decentralised policies to be extracted from centralised training.
Introduces a mixing network with non-negative weights to enforce monotonicity between joint and per-agent value functions, guaranteeing consistency between centralised and decentralised policies.
Uses hypernetworks to condition the mixing network on global state information available only during training, allowing richer representations than previous methods like VDN.
Introduces the StarCraft Multi-Agent Challenge (SMAC) as a new, challenging benchmark for deep multi-agent RL with decentralised execution.
Open-sources PyMARL, a modular and extensible framework for deep multi-agent RL research.

methods: Actor-Attention-Critic (AAC): an actor-critic algorithm for multi-agent reinforcement learning with a centralized critic using an attention mechanism to select relevant information for each agent at each timestep
problems: Scalability of multi-agent reinforcement learning to large numbers of agents
Learning effective decentralized policies in multi-agent settings with non-stationary and non-Markovian environments

novelty_claims: Proposes a centralized critic with an attention mechanism that dynamically selects which agents to attend to at each timestep, improving learning in complex multi-agent environments
The input space of the critic increases linearly with the number of agents, as opposed to quadratically in previous approaches
The method is applicable to cooperative, competitive, and mixed environments, exceeding the capability of prior work focused only on cooperative settings
No assumptions are made about the action spaces of the agents, making the approach broadly applicable

Stochastic role embedding space
Two novel regularizers for role identifiability and specialization
Conditioning individual policies on emergent roles
Variational estimation for learning role representations
problems: Inefficiency of existing MARL methods in complex tasks due to lack of structured role sharing

novelty_claims: Proposes a framework (ROMA) where roles are emergent rather than predefined, allowing for dynamic and adaptive role assignment in MARL.
Introduces two novel regularizers to ensure roles are both identifiable by behavior and specialized for sub-tasks.
Conditions agent policies on stochastic, learned role embeddings, enabling adaptive policy sharing among agents with similar roles.
Demonstrates that emergent roles improve team efficiency and push forward the state of the art in cooperative MARL.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["QMIX: Monotonic Value Function Factorisation for Deep Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning","Mixing network that combines per-agent value functions in a non-linear, monotonic way","Centralised training with decentralised execution paradigm"],"problems":["Learning decentralised policies for cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning under partial observability and communication constraints","Efficiently representing and learning joint action-value functions that allow decentralised execution","Overcoming the limitations of independent Q-learning (IQL), value decomposition networks (VDN), and fully centralised critics (e.g., COMA) in multi-agent RL"],"datasets":["StarCraft II micromanagement tasks (various maps, e.g., 5 Marines, 2 Stalkers & 3 Zealots)"],"metrics":["Win rate (performance on StarCraft II micromanagement tasks)","Learning speed (convergence rate)"],"results":[{"metric":"Win rate (StarCraft II micromanagement tasks)","value":"QMIX significantly outperforms IQL and VDN across all tested tasks"},{"metric":"Learning speed","value":"QMIX learns faster than IQL and VDN, especially on tasks with heterogeneous agents"}],"novelty_claims":["QMIX introduces a novel monotonic value function factorisation that allows for a richer class of centralised action-value functions than VDN, while still enabling decentralised execution.","QMIX uses a mixing network with enforced monotonicity to combine per-agent value functions, allowing tractable maximisation and consistency between centralised and decentralised policies.","QMIX is the first value-based method to combine centralised training with decentralised execution using a non-linear, monotonic mixing of agent value functions."]}

methods: QMIX: Monotonic Value Function Factorisation for Deep Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
Mixing network that combines per-agent value functions in a non-linear, monotonic way
Centralised training with decentralised execution paradigm
problems: Learning decentralised policies for cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning under partial observability and communication constraints
Efficiently representing and learning joint action-value functions that allow decentralised execution
Overcoming the limitations of independent Q-learning (IQL), value decomposition networks (VDN), and fully centralised critics (e.g., COMA) in multi-agent RL
datasets: StarCraft II micromanagement tasks (various maps, e.g., 5 Marines, 2 Stalkers & 3 Zealots)
metrics: Win rate (performance on StarCraft II micromanagement tasks)

results.value: QMIX significantly outperforms IQL and VDN across all tested tasks
QMIX learns faster than IQL and VDN, especially on tasks with heterogeneous agents

novelty_claims: QMIX introduces a novel monotonic value function factorisation that allows for a richer class of centralised action-value functions than VDN, while still enabling decentralised execution.
QMIX uses a mixing network with enforced monotonicity to combine per-agent value functions, allowing tractable maximisation and consistency between centralised and decentralised policies.
QMIX is the first value-based method to combine centralised training with decentralised execution using a non-linear, monotonic mixing of agent value functions.

methods: Systematic review of literature

Lack of comprehensive understanding of how PMIs affect peers without disabilities at the secondary level

novelty_claims: This is the first systematic review to comprehensively examine how peer-mediated interventions impact students without disabilities at the secondary level.

methods: Deployment and evaluation of an Intelligent Pedagogical Agent (IPA) in a real-world open-ended game design (GD) learning environment (Unity-CT) for K-12 students
Comparison of three intervention conditions: no intervention, 1-shot intervention, and repeated intervention by the IPA
Analysis of student behaviors and performance in response to IPA interventions targeting two specific error types in Unity-CT
problems: How to effectively deliver personalized assistance in open-ended game design environments to foster computational thinking in young students
Determining the impact of repeated versus single or no IPA interventions on student behavior and performance in OELEs
Addressing the challenge of providing timely, non-intrusive, and effective help in remote, online learning settings for young students
datasets: Interaction logs and behavioral data from students using Unity-CT in UME Academy's online classes (April to August 2021)
metrics: Number of errors made by students after intervention
Number of correct behaviors performed after intervention
Student perceptions of the IPA (liking, helpfulness, distraction, confusion rates)

results.metric: Number of errors (for one error type) after repeated IPA intervention
results.value: Significantly fewer errors compared to 1-shot or no intervention

results.metric: Number of correct behaviors (for one error type) after repeated IPA intervention
results.value: Significantly more correct behaviors compared to no intervention
Most students reported liking the IPA, found it helpful and not distracting
Repeated IPA generated more confusion than 1-shot, but overall confusion rates were low

novelty_claims: First investigation of Intelligent Pedagogical Agents in free-form game design activities for teaching computational thinking to young students
Implementation and evaluation of an IPA in a real-world, commercial, remote OELE (Unity-CT), as opposed to research-only or ad-hoc environments
Focus on the effect of repeated IPA interventions for a young audience not proficient in computers, whereas prior work focused on older students
Provision of insights into how IPA intervention repetition affects student behavior and performance in open-ended, remote learning environments

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Measurement of serum and urinary midkine, NGAL, and cystatin C levels at multiple time points before and after PCI using commercially available kits","Assessment of serum creatinine before and at 24 and 48 hours after PCI","Definition of contrast-induced nephropathy (CIN) as an increase in serum creatinine by >25% of baseline at 48 hours post-PCI"],"problems":["Early detection of contrast-induced acute kidney injury (CIAKI) in patients undergoing percutaneous coronary interventions (PCI)","Identification of reliable early biomarkers for CIAKI"],"datasets":["89 patients with normal serum creatinine undergoing PCI"],"metrics":["Serum midkine levels at various time points","Serum and urinary NGAL levels","Cystatin C levels","Serum creatinine levels","Prevalence of CIN (defined as >25% increase in serum creatinine at 48 hours)","Amount of contrast agent used"],"results":[{"metric":"Serum midkine increase at 2 hours post-PCI (P value)","value":"P < 0.001"},{"metric":"Prevalence of CIN","value":"10%"},{"metric":"Contrast agent amount in CIN vs non-CIN patients (P value)","value":"P < 0.05"},{"metric":"Serum midkine levels at 2, 4, and 8 hours in CIN patients","value":"Significantly higher than non-CIN patients"}],"novelty_claims":["This is the first study to investigate midkine as an early biomarker for contrast-induced acute kidney injury in patients undergoing PCI.","Midkine rises significantly earlier than serum creatinine, suggesting its potential as an early marker for renal ischemia and/or nephrotoxicity."]}

methods: Measurement of serum and urinary midkine, NGAL, and cystatin C levels at multiple time points before and after PCI using commercially available kits
Assessment of serum creatinine before and at 24 and 48 hours after PCI
Definition of contrast-induced nephropathy (CIN) as an increase in serum creatinine by >25% of baseline at 48 hours post-PCI
problems: Early detection of contrast-induced acute kidney injury (CIAKI) in patients undergoing percutaneous coronary interventions (PCI)
Identification of reliable early biomarkers for CIAKI
datasets: 89 patients with normal serum creatinine undergoing PCI
metrics: Serum midkine levels at various time points
Prevalence of CIN (defined as >25% increase in serum creatinine at 48 hours)

results.metric: Serum midkine increase at 2 hours post-PCI (P value)
Contrast agent amount in CIN vs non-CIN patients (P value)
Serum midkine levels at 2, 4, and 8 hours in CIN patients
results.value: Significantly higher than non-CIN patients

novelty_claims: This is the first study to investigate midkine as an early biomarker for contrast-induced acute kidney injury in patients undergoing PCI.
Midkine rises significantly earlier than serum creatinine, suggesting its potential as an early marker for renal ischemia and/or nephrotoxicity.
